# Resource Governance and Shared Infrastructure {#sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This appendix gives an adoption rubric for resources, identifies gaps in shared infrastructure for AI-assisted architecture research, and separates stable guidance from the living registry. A useful resource advances an architecture study, states its limits, names its maintainer, and documents how its current version will be updated or retired.}
```

This appendix judges resources by the architecture work they advance. Links
change, tools age, and benchmark versions move; the stable question is which
part of that work a resource serves. Does it help formulate a
question, explore meaningful alternatives, implement a checkable artifact,
evaluate a claim, test a mechanism explanation, or defend a bounded decision?
What interfaces, evidence, and failure modes does it provide, and what stronger
claim remains outside its evidentiary reach?

Specific tools, datasets, benchmarks, and projects change too quickly for a
print catalog to stay current. Their current links and availability observations
belong in the maintained registry. This appendix keeps the stable tests for
inclusion, adoption, comparison, and retirement.

> **Architecture 2.0 resource.** A resource is useful for Architecture 2.0 when
> it advances one or more capabilities in an architecture study, makes an
> architectural claim easier to test or review, or supplies shared
> infrastructure that lets studies become comparable and cumulative.

## Resource Adoption Test

A resource earns inclusion because of the architecture work it supports, not
because it is popular or describes itself as AI-enabled. Apply the five checks
in @tbl-resource-adoption-test. A synthesis wrapper that still requires an
architect to interpret raw logs, for example, may expose a tool API without
returning structured observations to the study.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{22\baselineskip}
```

| **Check** | **What an acceptable record states** | **Reason to reject or narrow the entry** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Architecture contribution** | Which study capabilities it advances (formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, or defense) and which claim it helps test. | The entry is only a generic AI product, directory listing, or vendor feature page. |
| **Interface and evidence** | Inputs, outputs, legal actions, feedback or evidence produced, failure behavior, versions, and material dependencies. | The resource hides semantics needed to interpret its result. |
| **Adoption boundary** | Workloads, technologies, assumptions, calibration limits, disclosure class, and strongest claim the resource cannot support. | A local score is presented as a general architecture result. |
| **Availability and verification** | Primary URL, artifact status, owner or steward, and the date a maintainer last verified the public record. | The link is dead, identity is ambiguous, or availability is only asserted by a submitter. |
| **Lifecycle** | Version or release identity, update path, deprecation or retirement state, and replacement or archive pointer when applicable. | The resource changes silently or obsolete evidence remains current by default. |

: **Criteria for adopting a resource.** A useful record says what
architecture work the resource advances, what evidence and failures it reports,
who maintains it, and when it should no longer be used. {#tbl-resource-adoption-test tbl-colwidths="[19,39,32]"}

For a proposed resource, record the task it supports, its version and
maintainer, the actions or feedback it exposes, documented failure modes, and
the strongest claim it can help support. If it cannot supply a required
capability or piece of evidence, add a complementary resource or narrow the
study's claim. If its identity, version, or limits cannot be established, do
not adopt it. Use
@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping to define the study and
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card to record it, then apply the adoption test to
each resource selected for that work.

## Shared Infrastructure for Cumulative Studies

@sec-data-representations-world-models explains why architecture data must
preserve connections among questions, candidate designs, tool observations, and
decisions rather than leave each artifact isolated. @sec-what-architect-owns
explains why shared resources also need comparison rules, review paths,
teaching resources, and, when promised, support for replay or reproduction.
@tbl-field-agenda-infrastructure turns those requirements into eight areas. For
each area, it states the minimum contents, the architecture work those contents
enable, and the failure that appears when support is missing or incomplete.

Adjacent communities already provide precedents for some rows. Judge a current
resource by whether its version, interfaces, evidence, ownership, and review
rules supply the listed contents for an AI-assisted architecture study.

A repository and an independent evaluation organization are not the same resource. A
repository can store datasets, code, reports, and failed runs. It does not by
itself define neutral baselines, control conflicts of interest, grant access to
protected evidence, audit accepts and rejects, retire obsolete versions, or
decide what resolves a disagreement. A community laboratory, consortium, or
federated review process can perform those functions when it records ownership,
rules, versions, disclosure boundaries, and an appeal path. It may use a
repository, but storage alone cannot provide independent evaluation.

No single project needs every row in this inventory. Across projects, weak or
missing support in one area limits which comparisons and cumulative claims
researchers can make. The living registry can point to current implementations,
but a registry entry cannot substitute for a review process that no institution
operates.

| **Infrastructure area** | **Minimum concrete contents** | **Capability added** | **Failure if absent** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Datasets, benchmarks, and paired multi-fidelity trajectories | Versioned architecture questions, baselines, workloads, candidate and evidence IDs, proxy and stronger-tool observations, tool and model versions, costs, uncertainty, failed stages, mechanism interpretations, bounded findings or decisions, redaction metadata, and alignment across fidelity levels. | Supports formulation, exploration, evaluation, and explanation by showing when cheap feedback predicts stronger evidence and when candidate rankings reverse at a stronger evidence level. | Isolated artifacts or log dumps cannot show which question was asked, when a proxy stopped tracking the design, why the result occurred, or what decision followed. |
| Versioned environments and tool contracts | Action and observation schemas, legal and invalid states, cost and fidelity labels, tool versions, dependencies, nondeterminism controls, failure semantics, proprietary boundaries, and deprecation rules. | Makes implementation and method evaluation comparable while making tool drift diagnosable. | Wrappers hide changed semantics, unsupported actions, or failures that alter the result. |
| Methods and reference practices | Role-appropriate AI and conventional methods, matched no-AI baselines, AI-role ablations, declared feedback budgets, experimental protocols, failure analysis, and reference reporting practices. | Lets a study attribute gains to the method, representation, tool path, or added feedback rather than an unmatched setup. | A method appears better because it receives more feedback, different baselines, hidden repair, or a more favorable environment. |
| Evaluation and neutral review | Held-out tasks, cross-tool calibration, uncertainty and total-cost reporting, independent execution or authorized review, common challenge procedures, matched audits of accepts and rejects, conflict disclosures, retest rules, escalation, and appeal. | Lets reviewers compare methods and narrow or settle bounded, disputed claims under shared rules. | A repository collects competing artifacts, but no accepted process can resolve the disagreement. |
| Reporting and claim interfaces | Compact study indexes, explicit claim and non-claim summaries, claim-to-evidence mapping, configs, seeds, commands, logs, failed and rejected alternatives, mechanism tests, integrity and disclosure manifests, decisions with named owners, and replay packages when replay is claimed. | Supports inspection by default and replay or reproduction when the released packet is sufficient. | A plausible result has no traceable path back to the question, execution, explanation, or decision. |
| Standards, governance, and community institutions | Stable interface and evidence semantics, named maintainers, version ownership, integrity checks, waiver expiration, drift monitoring, revalidation, retirement, incident-driven reopening, conflict rules, and ownership transfer. | Keeps records and evaluations meaningful across changing tools, models, workloads, and maintainers. | Links decay, rules drift silently, and obsolete evidence continues to be treated as support for current claims. |
| Incentives and confidentiality | Publication and artifact credit, benchmark status, incentives for industrial participation in neutral evaluation, protected evaluation paths, disclosure rules, and credit for useful failure records. | Rewards teams for maintaining useful evidence while respecting legitimate intellectual-property and disclosure boundaries. | Teams rationally withhold failures and protected evidence, leaving public comparisons incomplete or distorted. |
| Education | Courses, executable labs, capstones, review exercises, and mentoring aligned to formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense, including mechanism testing, AI-contribution evaluation, redaction, and scoped delegation. | Lets new architects conduct and challenge bounded AI-assisted studies instead of stopping at the vocabulary. | Tacit practice remains private, tools become recipes without judgment, and learners cannot transfer the practice to a new problem. |

: **Infrastructure needed for cumulative research.** Datasets, environments, methods, evaluation, reporting, institutions, incentives, and education are all needed for comparable and reviewable AI-assisted architecture studies. {#tbl-field-agenda-infrastructure tbl-colwidths="[20,34,22,18]"}

These areas work together. A benchmark without a versioned environment cannot
support a stable comparison, and a replay package without a review path cannot
settle a disputed claim. Each resource record should name which infrastructure
functions it provides.

## Stable Material and Executable Practice

The following material supports recurring study tasks, and its role does not
depend on a current tool URL.

- **Bootstrap playbook.** @sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping provides the sequence for
  starting an architecture study.
- **Design-loop card and review rubric.** @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card and
  @tbl-blank-design-loop-card provide the reusable review artifact.
- **Executed study and replay.** @sec-running-the-loop reports one recorded
  model-assisted study, a matched conventional arm, twelve systolic-array
  simulations with SCALE-Sim 3.0.0, a held-out mechanism test, and a
  network-free replay. Its scope stops at the simulator evidence it actually
  produced.
- **Runnable companion labs.** [Architecture 2.0 labs](https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2/tree/main/labs)
  let readers construct environment contracts, record decisions, and replay
  studies.

Use the playbook and card to define and review a study. Treat the executed study
and labs as scoped examples, and check their tool versions, execution paths, and
evidence boundaries before adapting them to a new claim.

## Living Registry Boundary

Stable resource roles and adoption boundaries stay in this appendix. Current
tool, dataset, benchmark, and project links belong in the searchable registry at
[arch2.mlsysbook.ai/tools/](https://arch2.mlsysbook.ai/tools/), where maintainers
can update or retire them without revising the main text. Use the registry to
locate a candidate resource and see when a maintainer last checked its public
record. Before adopting it, verify the version, interfaces, evidence, limits,
and update or retirement status against the primary project. Cite the primary
paper or project artifact for scholarly claims.

The registry makes five governance details explicit:

- **Owner:** Maintainers of the Architecture 2.0 repository review source
  records and generated output.
- **Inclusion:** An entry must fit a fixed architecture-resource category,
  provide a primary URL and concise identity, state its architecture-study
  contribution, and survive maintainer review. Availability labels report what
  a maintainer observed; they are not endorsements of technical fitness.
- **Update:** Each entry has a `last_verified` date. Source records live in
  `tools/registry/`, and the generated `tools/tools.yml` index must match them.
- **Retirement:** Broken, superseded, misleading, or out-of-scope entries are
  corrected or removed from the live index through review. Repository history
  and frozen release snapshots preserve the prior public state.
- **Print snapshot:** This release freezes the registry as of July 14, 2026 in
  the [archived YAML snapshot](https://arch2.mlsysbook.ai/tools/snapshots/registry-2026-07-14.yml).

The [Architecture 2.0 GitHub repository](https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2)
accepts registry contributions and executable artifacts. It does not endorse or
independently evaluate submissions. Any such status requires a review process
with the controls described in @tbl-field-agenda-infrastructure.

## Using and Citing This Material

In paper reviews, project proposals, class discussions, and internal design
reviews, use the adoption test to state what each resource contributes and where
its evidence stops. Cite the publication and its edition when referring to this
framework, and cite the primary resource when making a claim about a tool,
dataset, benchmark, or method.

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
The online edition displays a version identifier. Include that identifier and
the site URL when citing material that may change between printings.
:::

A resource can make an architectural claim easier to test, but its presence
does not establish the claim. The study must still report the evidence, limits,
and resulting finding or decision.
